title: The Sword & Sorcery Saga - The Dark World, by Henry Kuttner
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kFWumpkL5yI

Daniel (@dungeondive) explores Henry Kuttner's The Dark World, a 1946 sword & planet tale where protagonist Edward Bond becomes trapped sharing his body with an evil alternate-dimension sorcerer named Ganalon. Rather than following the expected hero narrative, the story unfolds from Ganalon's perspective as he discovers his role ruling the Coven—a faction of supernatural beings. Daniel finds the novel's central conceit intriguing: the war between science and superstition, where magic is revealed as biological mutation.

Though Daniel appreciates the book's conceptual premise and writing quality, he finds it lacks the momentum and geographic scope he craves in fantasy adventure. The story dwells too long in political intrigue between warring factions without sufficient character development or exploration of the Dark World itself. The abundance of exclamation points and oversized cast of underdeveloped characters further dilutes the narrative. However, it remains a worthwhile exploration of Kuttner's versatility beyond his acclaimed Atlantean-era stories.

----

How important is world exploration and travel to your enjoyment of sword & sorcery? Does the focus on political intrigue instead appeal to you?
